clinical_history: 30-yr-old man, not on any medication. What waveform is prominent? What is the likely diagnosis, assuming prior normal ECG?
diagnosis: There are very (!) prominent U waves with Q-T or QT(U) prolongation. He had severe hypokalemia (1.5 mEq/L) due to diarrhea. Calcium was normal, as was magnesium (remember pure hypocalcemia prolongs the ST segment primarily but does not give large U waves.) Obviously, this acquired-type long-QT(U) syndrome puts the subject at risk for torsade de pointes which he fortunately did not have before coming to hospital. The same type ECG may occur with drug-related QT(U) prolongation syndromes and with hereditary long QT(U) syndromes related to "channelopathies," including the previousl designated, classic Romano-Ward and Jervell Lange-Nielsen syndromes.

report.intervals: PR: 160 ms, QRS: 80 ms, QT(U): ~600 ms, QTc: ~670 ms
report.findings: Very prominent U waves, prolonged QT(U) interval, mild ST depression in inferior and lateral leads.
report.comparison: New changes compared to assumed prior normal EKG.
report.impression: Acquired long QT(U) syndrome due to severe hypokalemia (1.5 mEq/L), posing a risk for torsade de pointes.
